items.mistake: Using tanh for binary classification output probabilities
items.fix: Use sigmoid on the output layer so ŷ ∈ (0, 1) represents P(y = 1). Tanh outputs can be negative and are not valid probabilities.
items.why: BCE and probabilistic interpretation require (0, 1). Tanh is for zero-centered hidden or signed bounded outputs, not Bernoulli probabilities.
items.example: Wrong: output = torch.tanh(logits); loss = BCE(output, y). Right: output = torch.sigmoid(logits) or BCEWithLogitsLoss(logits, y).

items.mistake: Stacking many tanh layers without controlling pre-activation scale
items.fix: Use Xavier/Glorot initialization, batch/layer normalization, or switch hidden layers to ReLU for depth. Keep pre-activations near zero early in training.
items.why: Large z drives tanh into saturation where gradient ≈ 0, blocking learning in deep stacks.
items.example: Five tanh layers with default large weights: most activations ±0.99, loss flatlines. Re-init with Xavier or add LayerNorm after linear.

items.mistake: Assuming tanh and sigmoid are interchangeable because both are S-curves
items.fix: Pick by range and role: tanh for zero-centered hidden states (−1, 1); sigmoid for [0, 1] gates and probabilities. See canonical comparison in this topic.
items.why: Different output ranges change gradient flow, bias, and downstream loss compatibility.
items.example: RNN hidden: tanh. LSTM forget gate: sigmoid. Swapping them breaks the intended [0,1] gating semantics.

items.mistake: Implementing tanh with naive exp for large inputs
items.fix: Use framework builtins (np.tanh, torch.tanh) that handle overflow via stable formulas.
items.why: e^x overflows for large positive x in naive (exp(x)-exp(-x))/(exp(x)+exp(-x)) implementations.
items.example: Wrong: manual formula on x=500 → inf/nan. Right: np.tanh(500) → 1.0 safely.
